title: Convex chosen as backend over custom WebSocket infrastructure
position: Use Convex as the backend for Provenance
rationale: Convex provides reactive queries, server-side functions, real-time sync, and document-based storage without building custom WebSocket infrastructure. First-party WorkOS integration simplifies auth. Trade-off: 1MB document size limit requires normalized schema with separate tables instead of embedded sub-documents.
context: Provenance requires real-time collaborative editing where multiple users see changes instantly. Options considered: custom WebSocket server, Firebase/Supabase, Convex.

title: ELK layout engine chosen over Dagre for graph visualization
position: Use ELK (Eclipse Layout Kernel) for automatic graph layout
rationale: ELK supports hierarchical layouts with zone partitioning (grouping nodes by type into visual regions), handles large graphs efficiently, and integrates with React Flow. Dagre lacks zone support and has less sophisticated routing. D3-force produces aesthetically pleasing but unpredictable layouts unsuitable for structured requirement hierarchies.
context: The canvas UI needs automatic graph layout for requirement trees with zone partitioning. Options evaluated: Dagre, ELK, D3-force, custom layout.

title: Focus+Context selected as primary depth view after evaluating 8 prototypes
position: Use Focus+Context as the primary depth visualization approach
rationale: Focus+Context maintains spatial context while allowing deep exploration of subtrees. It handles the recursive nature of requirements naturally -- the focused node expands in detail while surrounding nodes remain visible but compact. Alternative approaches (fisheye, treemap, indented tree, sunburst, etc.) either lost spatial context or couldn't handle the mixed-type graph structure.
context: The UI needs a way to show the full requirement tree at depth while maintaining context of where the user is. 8 prototype approaches were evaluated for the depth view.

title: Recursive requirements replace initiative/epic/story hierarchy
position: Use a single Requirement type that recurses to arbitrary depth via parentId
rationale: Requirements compose recursively via bind (refinement produces sub-requirements). The hierarchy IS the recursion -- no separate concepts needed. This follows the monad pattern: each requirement is a unit of work that can be decomposed further. Status flows naturally from leaves to root. Cross-domain sharing works via edges (depends_on, contradicts) between any nodes in the tree.
context: Traditional project management uses distinct container types (initiative, epic, story, task). Provenance needed a way to decompose work at arbitrary depth without artificial tier limits.

title: One-directional sync from Provenance to Broker for rule definitions
position: Provenance syncs rules to the broker (one-directional). The broker owns contract-to-rule linkage and coverage tracking.
rationale: The broker is the enforcement engine and needs rule definitions pushed to it. Coverage data flows back as read-only queries. This prevents circular dependencies: Provenance is the source of truth for rule definitions and provenance chains, the broker is the source of truth for runtime enforcement and coverage. Hourly cron job handles retries and coverage refresh.
context: Rules are discovered in Provenance but enforced by the Statesman broker. The sync direction and ownership boundary needed to be established.

title: Static analysis first, runtime reporters later
position: Start with static analysis. Design the annotation format and Provenance API to support runtime evidence later, but ship the scanner first.
rationale: Static analysis delivers the core value (traceability link from rule to test) with a single tool vs N framework integrations. It establishes the annotation convention that runtime reporters will reuse. The link itself is valuable even without pass/fail -- when a regulation changes, knowing which tests cover the affected rule is immediately actionable. Runtime reporters add richer signal but require per-framework maintenance (Jest, pytest, JUnit, Go) and higher adoption friction. Build the foundation first, enrich later.
context: External test coverage linking needs a delivery strategy. Two approaches: (1) static analysis scanner that extracts annotations from source code without running tests, or (2) runtime test reporters (per-framework plugins) that capture pass/fail results. Both use the same annotation format.

title: Mirror Statesman contract metadata vocabulary in external test annotations
position: Use Allure's label/link primitive architecture but populate it with Statesman's existing vocabulary: name, description, tags from Contract trait; rule, coverage, confidence, notes from RuleContract. No epic/feature/story.
rationale: Consistency: coverage data looks the same whether it comes from a Statesman contract or an annotated external test. The Provenance coverage model doesn't need to distinguish sources -- the fields are identical. No new concepts to learn for teams already using Statesman contracts. Non-technical viewers in Provenance see the same metadata (description, tags, coverage level) regardless of how coverage was established. Avoids positioning Statesman as a PM tool.
context: External test annotations need a vocabulary for describing tests and linking them to rules. Three options considered: (1) invent new annotation-specific fields, (2) adopt Allure's epic/feature/story hierarchy, (3) replicate the fields that Statesman contracts already carry. Allure's label/link primitive model is sound, but Allure's organizational labels (epic/feature/story) are project management concepts that conflict with Statesman's strategic positioning.

title: External test coverage data lives in the broker, not Provenance
position: Store external test coverage in the broker via RuleContract with mapping_source=imported. Provenance continues to pull from one source.
rationale: Single source of truth for all coverage data. No merge logic needed in Provenance. Existing coverage queries and gaps analysis get richer for free. Future broker expansion (risk scoring, drift detection, transaction correlation) benefits from having external test evidence already co-located. The broker does not need to care how coverage was established -- the mapping_source field already distinguishes Manual/Inferred/Imported. High likelihood of expanding broker scope to further leverage external test data.
context: External test coverage needs a home. Options: (1) Provenance (Convex) with a new table, merging with broker contract coverage at read time, (2) Broker, using the existing RuleContract table with mapping_source=imported, (3) Both. The broker is the enforcement authority and already has the coverage query surface (/rules/coverage, /rules/gaps). Provenance already pulls coverage from the broker via fetchRuleCoverage.

title: Simple key-value comment syntax, migrate to native annotations later if needed
position: Use simple @statesman key: value syntax. If native annotations are needed later, a statesman migrate command handles the mechanical transformation.
rationale: Comments should look like comments. The migration from one text format to another is trivial -- a sed one-liner or a CLI migrate command. Don't optimize the comment format for a future that may not come. The simple format is easier to type, easier to read, and easier to parse. Native annotations are a separate concern with their own syntax per language.
context: The annotation format lives in comments. Two syntax options: (1) function-call style like @statesman.rule("CODE") which mirrors what native decorators would look like, or (2) simple key-value like @statesman rule: CODE which is honest about being metadata in a comment. Function-call style has the advantage of being forward-compatible with native annotations but adds visual noise (parens, quotes) to something that is not code.

title: Use tree-sitter for AST-based annotation scanning instead of line-oriented regex
position: Use tree-sitter with grammars statically linked. Optimize binary size later if needed.
rationale: Tree-sitter solves comment-to-code association, function detection, and block boundaries without custom heuristics per language. Adding a language is adding a grammar, not writing a parser. Foundation compounds -- future features (function signature extraction, module structure, import analysis) come for free. 22 MB for 55 grammars is acceptable for a CLI tool (kubectl is 50 MB). The industry universally uses AST parsing for production comment scanners. Optimize later with feature flags or dynamic loading if binary size becomes a concern.
context: The scanner needs to associate @statesman annotations with test functions. Two approaches: (1) line-oriented regex parser with comment-prefix detection per language and heuristics for block boundaries, (2) tree-sitter AST parsing which provides comment-to-code association, function detection, and block boundaries for free. Industry standard for comment-based annotation scanning is AST parsing (JSDoc, ESLint, Doxygen all use it). Line-oriented regex is simpler but fragile -- edge cases with decorators, blank lines, nested classes. Tree-sitter adds ~10-15 MB for 6 grammars or ~22 MB for 55 grammars.

title: External tests as lightweight evidence records, not nullable FKs
position: Option 3: Create external test evidence records. RuleContract links to them the same way it links to contract versions. An external test is a form of coverage evidence -- it just is not a Statesman contract.
rationale: Keeps the RuleContract FK clean -- no nullable FKs, no ambiguity. The coverage query surface (coverage, gaps, rule_contracts) works unchanged because external tests are linked the same way. The evidence record holds the test metadata the scanner extracted (file, line, function, name, description, tags) which is useful context when viewing coverage. Option 1 ships faster but muddies the data model. Option 2 splits the coverage model into two tables. Option 3 is the honest representation: external tests are evidence, and evidence links to rules.
context: RuleContract currently requires contract_version_id (FK to a Statesman contract). External tests aren't contracts. Three options: (1) make contract_version_id nullable for imported mappings, (2) new table for non-contract coverage, (3) create thin external test evidence records that the coverage system links to like contract versions.

title: Agent tokens inherit project scope from parent by default
position: Agents inherit the project access of whoever created them. Explicit project_ids can narrow scope but the default is inheritance.
rationale: Matches least-surprise principle. Agent created by a human gets the same project access as that human. Explicit narrowing available for CI/CD use cases.
context: Agent tokens need project scoping. Should they default to all org projects or require explicit selection?

title: Agents cannot approve resolutions by default, configurable via custom roles
position: Default role sets (analyst, contributor) exclude resolutions:approve. Custom role sets can include it if the org explicitly opts in.
rationale: Resolution approval is a human judgment call by default. But the permission system should not hard-code this — org admins can override via custom WorkOS roles.
context: Should AI agents be able to approve resolutions? This is a human oversight question.

title: Broker stays simple with read/write scopes; fine-grained permissions are a Provenance concern
position: Keep the broker authorization simple (read/write). Fine-grained per-artifact-type permissions are enforced only at the Provenance layer.
rationale: The broker is a thin proxy — you either read things or update things. This matches Pact's two-level model. Adding fine-grained checks to the broker would duplicate Provenance logic.
context: Should the broker enforce fine-grained permissions (requirements:create) or keep its simple read/write model?

title: Agent tokens require mandatory expiration with rotation mechanism
position: Agent tokens must have mandatory expiration (expires_at NOT NULL) and a rotation command from day one.
rationale: Security best practice. Prevents indefinite credential exposure. Token rotation is already a known gap in the human API key system.
context: Token rotation is already a pain point for human API keys. How should agent tokens handle lifecycle?

title: Use WorkOS RBAC directly instead of custom role_sets table
position: Use WorkOS RBAC directly. Define roles and permissions in WorkOS Dashboard. The control plane is a convenience layer, not a role authority. No custom role_sets table needed.
rationale: No vendor lock-in concern — WorkOS is set-and-forget for this project. WorkOS RBAC is free up to 1M MAU, mature, and already in our SDK. Building custom RBAC duplicates what WorkOS provides.
context: Should Statesman build its own RBAC system or integrate with WorkOS roles and permissions?

title: Support agent-to-agent delegation with permission narrowing
position: Agents can create delegation tokens for other agents with strictly narrower permissions. The delegation chain is traceable via created_by_agent_id back to the human owner.
rationale: Enables orchestration patterns where a primary agent delegates specific tasks to sub-agents with least-privilege tokens. Permission narrowing prevents privilege escalation.
context: Can one agent create tokens for another agent? What are the constraints?

title: Deleted agents keep Convex user records for historical attribution
position: Keep the record. Agent Convex user records are retained for historical attribution. No soft-delete mechanism needed.
rationale: The goal is to preserve what the agent was, what access it had, and who created it. Provenance is an audit trail — deleting attribution records defeats the purpose.
context: When an agent is deleted, what happens to its synthetic Convex user record?

title: Defer rate limiting for agents
position: Defer. Rate limiting is not a concern right now. Can be added later as a feature if needed.
rationale: Premature optimization. No current abuse vector. The metering infrastructure (agent_id, last_active_at, audit log) will be in place when rate limiting becomes necessary.
context: Should agents have rate limits distinct from human users?

title: Broker authentication pipeline enforcement backfill
position: Each pattern should be tracked as a Provenance rule: BROKER-AUTH-001 through BROKER-AUTH-006
rationale: Authentication is the first layer of defense. These rules enforce Bearer-only extraction, token type discrimination, RS256 JWT validation, control plane delegation, hashed cache keys, and JWKS refresh rate limiting.
context: Codebase scan identified 6 enforcement patterns in broker for authentication

title: Broker authorization and permission model backfill
position: Each pattern should be tracked as a Provenance rule: BROKER-AUTHZ-001 through BROKER-AUTHZ-006
rationale:
Authorization model gates every handler with type-safe permission extractors (RequirePermission

), hierarchical scopes (admin>write>read), mandatory auth extraction, proposal state machine enforcement, JWT permission merging, and 32 fine-grained permission slugs.
context: Codebase scan identified 6 enforcement patterns in broker for authorization

title: Broker multi-tenant isolation backfill
position: Each pattern should be tracked as a Provenance rule: BROKER-TENANT-001 through BROKER-TENANT-005
rationale: Tenant isolation is enforced at three layers (handler, service, SQL) with defense-in-depth. These rules cover project-scoped queries, project allowlist, explicit ownership verification, cross-org prevention, and body/auth consistency checks.
context: Codebase scan identified 5 enforcement patterns in broker for tenant isolation

title: Broker input validation and safety backfill
position: Each pattern should be tracked as a Provenance rule: BROKER-INPUT-001 through BROKER-INPUT-003
rationale: Input validation prevents malformed data and resource exhaustion. Progressive transaction validation, pagination limit caps, and startup config validation protect the system boundary.
context: Codebase scan identified 3 enforcement patterns in broker for input validation

title: Broker information leakage prevention backfill
position: Each pattern should be tracked as a Provenance rule: BROKER-LEAK-001 through BROKER-LEAK-003
rationale: Information leakage prevention uses 404-not-403 for cross-project access (anti-enumeration), token prefix-only logging, and generic RFC 7807 error titles to prevent information disclosure.
context: Codebase scan identified 3 enforcement patterns in broker for leakage prevention

title: Broker infrastructure security backfill
position: Each pattern should be tracked as a Provenance rule: BROKER-INFRA-001 through BROKER-INFRA-004
rationale: Infrastructure security enforces request timeouts (30s), fail-closed on control plane unavailability, PostgreSQL-only database protocol, and parameterized SQL queries across all 21 repository files.
context: Codebase scan identified 4 enforcement patterns in broker for infrastructure security

title: Broker audit trail and attribution backfill
position: Each pattern should be tracked as a Provenance rule: BROKER-AUDIT-001 through BROKER-AUDIT-003
rationale: Audit trail requires structured tracing with project_id in every handler span, identity attribution on all mutations (user/agent/service/api_key), and content-addressed deduplication for contract versions.
context: Codebase scan identified 3 enforcement patterns in broker for audit trail
